claim.hypotheses_location: Section 4 (The current research): “we predict that individuals under mortality salience will be less likely to give utilitarian responses to moral conflicts. We also predict that mortality salience will have no comparable effect on control scenarios…”
claim.statement_location: Abstract: “we predicted that individuals under mortality salience would be less likely to give utilitarian responses to moral conflicts. Two experiments corroborated this hypothesis.” Also see Section 4 (The current research) for explicit predictions.

data.source: Laboratory experiments with university participants making moral judgments on written scenarios
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 85 participants (59 women; mean age = 22.00, SD = 3.94). Experiment 2: 115 participants (84 women; mean age = 23.77, SD = 5.08); final analyzed N = 110 after removing 5 multivariate outliers.
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant judgments (percentage of utilitarian responses per participant across scenarios)
data.notes: Participants were randomly assigned to conditions. Scenario order was counterbalanced (eight questionnaire versions). Non-lethal harm versions of high-conflict moral dilemmas (e.g., Crying Baby, Captive Soldier) were used to avoid inducing mortality salience in controls. Experiment 2 included a Dot Memory Task to impose cognitive load (low: easy 3x3 matrices; high: difficult 3x3; very high: 4x4, with longer presentation).

method.description: Two experiments tested whether mortality salience reduces utilitarian moral judgments. Mortality salience was manipulated by having participants write about their own death (vs. extreme pain control). Participants then judged the moral acceptability of target actions in non-lethal harm dilemmas with conflict (utilitarian vs. deontic response conflict) and control versions (congruent responses). Experiment 2 additionally manipulated cognitive load via a spatial dot memory task at three levels (low, high, very high) to compare the effect of mortality salience to that of cognitive load. The primary outcome was the percentage of utilitarian responses, analyzed with ANOVAs and t-tests.
steps: Experiment 1: Recruit participants and randomly assign to Death (mortality salience) or Pain (control) conditions.
Administer mortality salience manipulation (two brief open-ended prompts about one’s own death vs. extreme pain).
Present four moral scenarios per participant: two non-lethal harm dilemmas (each with conflict and control versions); counterbalance order across participants.
Collect moral acceptability judgments; code utilitarian responses and compute percentage utilitarian responses.
Analyze with ANOVA (problem type × condition) and planned t-tests for conflict and control problems.
Experiment 2: Recruit new participants; randomly assign to three cognitive load levels (low, high, very high) and to Death vs. Pain conditions.
Implement Dot Memory Task before each moral problem to impose load (memorize dot matrices; reproduce after judgment).
Administer the same moral scenarios and collect judgments; record dot-memory performance.
Conduct ANOVA including problem type, condition (Death vs. Pain), and cognitive load; follow up with analyses focused on conflict problems, including post hoc comparisons among load levels.
method.models: ANOVAs with factors including problem type (conflict vs. control), condition (Death vs. Pain), and cognitive load (low, high, very high); planned t-tests; report partial eta squared effect sizes.
method.outcome_variable: Percentage of utilitarian responses (moral acceptability judgments coded as utilitarian vs. deontic), primarily on conflict problems
method.independent_variables: Mortality salience condition (Death vs. Pain), problem type (conflict vs. control), cognitive load level (low, high, very high; Experiment 2)
method.control_variables: Order of scenarios counterbalanced; scenarios restricted to non-lethal harm to avoid confounding mortality salience in controls; specific scenario content (Crying Baby, Captive Soldier) balanced within-subjects

results.summary: Across two experiments, mortality salience reduced the frequency of utilitarian responses on conflict moral dilemmas, with no comparable effect on control scenarios. In Experiment 1, a significant interaction indicated fewer utilitarian responses under mortality salience for conflict problems only. In Experiment 2, mortality salience again reduced utilitarian responses on conflict problems. Cognitive load reduced utilitarian responses only at a very high level, and the mortality salience effect was comparable in magnitude to this very high load. No interaction between mortality salience and cognitive load was detected on conflict problems.

metadata.original_paper_title: Mortality salience and morality: Thinking about death makes people less utilitarian
